profile.name: Jordan Lee
profile.household: Lives with long-term partner Alex, no kids yet
goals: Get promoted to Director of Product within 18 months
Be more present with partner Alex
pain_points: Overcommitted calendar — back-to-back meetings most days
Guilt about not spending enough quality time with Alex

sutras.text: Jordan's 2+ months of work disengagement is bleeding into relationship distance with Alex — early morning work thoughts, skipped gym sessions, and back-to-back meetings without lunch breaks are creating a presence deficit that's threatening his most important relationship.

Despite shipping features and receiving strong technical performance reviews, Jordan hasn't felt genuinely excited about a work problem in two months — suggesting his role has become mechanically competent but emotionally depleted.

themes.name: Financial Discipline vs Life Decision Paralysis

sutras.text: Jordan's financial systems are his most successful life area — consistent $1,200+ monthly savings toward his $30k house down payment — yet he's paralyzed on major life decisions like moving in with Alex that require emotional rather than mechanical execution.
Recurring budget planning sessions with Alex every few weeks reveal they're financially aligned but emotionally distant — they can plan money together but Jordan hasn't asked about her job in weeks.

themes.name: Leadership Development Blocked by Ambiguous Feedback

sutras.text: Jordan's promotion pursuit is stuck in a feedback loop — multiple conversations with his manager about the Director track but consistently ambiguous responses, while performance reviews highlight the exact leadership gap he needs to close.
Jordan feels more anxiety about his leadership skills than his technical delivery, yet his manager's feedback remains vague enough that he's updating his resume while pursuing internal promotion.

themes.name: Personal Systems Started But Never Sustained

sutras.text: Jordan has started building a personal OKR dashboard at least 4 different times across 18 months but never sustained usage — a pattern of creating productivity systems during stress periods then abandoning them when pressure mounts.

Jordan's health tracking shows consistent data collection (Fitbit notifications, running logs) but behavioral gaps — 5.8 hours average sleep despite 7+ hour goals, gym skipped after work calls run over.

Jordan and Alex are having regular conversations about their future (moving, finances) but he hasn't asked about her job in two weeks and barely says good morning — they're planning a life together while emotionally drifting apart.

Jordan's relationship repair attempts (emails about being more present, anniversary planning) remain unfinished while his work systems (roadmap docs, performance reviews) get completed — revealing where his attention actually flows.

top_insights: Jordan's financial discipline is masking deeper emotional avoidance — he can save $1,200/month but can't sustain a conversation about Alex's job or complete his promotion case
Two months of work disengagement is systematically undermining his relationship through presence deficits, early morning work anxiety, and mechanical rather than emotional engagement
Jordan's strongest systems (finances, fitness tracking, work delivery) operate independently of his actual life satisfaction and relationship quality

sutras.data_story: Jordan is a high-performing product manager whose financial discipline and technical delivery mask a deeper crisis of presence and emotional engagement. While he consistently saves toward life goals and ships work features, he's been emotionally disengaged from work for two months and increasingly distant from his partner Alex. His unfinished personal systems and stalled promotion pursuit suggest someone who starts strong but struggles to sustain effort when emotional rather than mechanical execution is required.

USER: We're thinking about buying a house. How do I even start figuring out if we can afford it? ASSISTANT: Start with the 28/36 rule: your housing payment shouldn't exceed 28% of gross monthly income, and total debt payments shouldn't exceed 36%. With your combined income you'd want to model different down payment scenarios. Beyond price, factor in property tax (Texas is high), HOA if applicable, maintenance buffer (1-2% of home value annually), and how long you plan to stay — buying makes less sense under 5 years typically. What's your timeline?

USER: I keep saying yes to things and then resenting it. How do I get better at saying no? ASSISTANT: Saying no is a skill, not a personality trait. The easiest starting move: don't answer immediately. 'Let me check my capacity and get back to you' buys you space to decide intentionally. Next, get clear on your top 3 priorities — anything outside them needs a compelling reason. What's the last thing you said yes to that you immediately regretted?

USER: Year-end reflection: what questions should I be asking myself? ASSISTANT: My favorites for a year-end: What did I start that I'm glad I started? What did I tolerate that I shouldn't have? Where did I grow in a way that surprised me? What do I want more of next year — not goals, just more of? And: who showed up for me, and did I show up for them? Take your time with these.
